issues.title: Agent input schemas are parsed but never validated at dispatch time
issues.body: AgentSpec.inputSchema is populated by agent-spec.ts:126 but step-agent.ts never validates the step's resolved input against it. The synthesizer agent requires [structure, quality, patterns] but resumable-analysis.workflow.yaml passes only structure and quality — this silently succeeds. Input schemas are decorative, creating false confidence in typed composition. Either add validation in executeAgentStep() before dispatch, or remove inputSchema from the type to stop pretending.
issues.resolved_by: v0.11.0 commit 92e5427 — inputSchema validation in executeAgentStep(), task-001/ver-001. Criterion 6 (audit all callers) deferred to task-002.

issues.title: Template composition (extends/blocks/macros) used in only 1 of 22 template families
issues.body: The Nunjucks template system supports extends, block, macro, and include — powerful composition primitives. Only the analyzers/ family uses them (structure/quality/patterns extending base-analyzer.md). The other 21 template directories are flat files with no composition. This means the template machinery is over-engineered relative to actual usage, OR the agent library is under-exploiting available composition. Agents like investigator, researcher, gap-identifier, and gap-resolution-assessor share structural patterns (read code → produce structured findings → validate against schema) that could share a base template.

issues.title: Agent reuse across workflows is structural repetition, not parametric recombination
issues.body: code-explorer appears in 6 workflows but always with identical input (path: ${{ input.path }}). structure-analyzer appears in 6 workflows, always with (exploration + path). synthesizer appears in 4 workflows with minor variation. Agents are copy-pasted at the YAML level — the typed input system and Nunjucks templates could enable genuinely different compositions (e.g., explorer with depth parameter, synthesizer with optional analysis channels) but this isn't happening. The agents should be redesigned to accept variable input shapes and adapt their prompts accordingly.

issues.title: No ad-hoc agent invocation path — every use requires a .workflow.yaml spec
issues.body: pi-subagents allows /run scout 'analyze auth' with zero files written. Our system requires a .workflow.yaml for every agent invocation. This makes the barrier to use much higher for exploration, debugging, and one-off tasks. A /workflow run-agent <name> command or equivalent tool call that dispatches a single agent step with inline input (JSON or key=value) would bridge this gap. The agent's input schema (once enforced) provides validation; the task template provides prompt rendering; the output schema provides structured results — all without a workflow spec.

issues.title: Parallel agent steps share working tree — filesystem conflicts in concurrent file-writing workflows
issues.body: The parallel: step type and DAG-inferred parallelism run agents concurrently in the same working directory. If two parallel agents modify the same file (or even different files that trigger build/lint side effects), results are undefined. pi-subagents solves this with git worktree isolation: temp worktrees per agent, node_modules symlink, diff capture after completion, cleanup in finally block. Our parallel steps need an optional worktree: true flag with equivalent machinery.

issues.title: No recursion depth guard for future nested workflow invocation (Phase 6)
issues.body: The workflow: field on StepSpec is parsed but rejected at validation time (Phase 6 — not yet implemented). When unblocked, nested workflow invocation will allow unbounded recursive spawning without a depth guard. pi-subagents' pattern: PI_SUBAGENT_MAX_DEPTH env var propagated via PI_SUBAGENT_DEPTH in child process env. Must be in place before workflow: steps are enabled.

issues.title: assigned_agent field on tasks is decorative — nothing routes work based on it
issues.body: The tasks schema has an assigned_agent field but no code reads it for dispatch routing. An agent could be assigned to a task and a different agent dispatched by the workflow. The execute-task workflow dispatches task-worker regardless of assigned_agent. Either the field should drive dispatch (workflow reads it and selects agent) or it should be removed to stop pretending.

issues.title: No automatic decision recording during agent execution — decisions block task field exists but nothing populates it
issues.body: The decisions schema has a task field (added v0.11.0) for backlink from decisions to the task during which the decision was made. But no agent, workflow step, or tool automatically records decisions during task execution. The execution-results schema has a decisions[] array in agent output but nothing routes those entries to the decisions block. Decisions made by agents during execute-task are lost.

issues.title: No phase-level verification rollup — individual task verification exists but nothing aggregates to phase success_criteria
issues.body: The verification schema supports target_type: phase but nothing produces phase-level verification entries. Phase specs have success_criteria with verify_method but no workflow connects individual task completion to phase-level verification. A phase could have all tasks completed and verified but no phase-level evidence that success_criteria were met.

issues.title: Issue lifecycle not connected to task completion — resolved_by must be set manually
issues.body: When task-001 was completed, issue-001 status remained open. No workflow or tool automatically updates issue status when the task that fixes it completes. The issues schema has resolved_by but nothing populates it from task/verification data. Manual intervention required to keep issues block in sync with reality.

issues.title: Agent execution metadata (model, cost, duration) not persisted to project blocks
issues.body: Workflow step results contain agent name, model, token usage, cost, and duration in run state (.workflows/runs/). But this data is ephemeral — not persisted to any project block. No record of which agent executed a task at what model tier with what quality. The verification block has evidence text but not structured execution metadata.

issues.title: Completion message fires on workflow failure — misleading success message
issues.body: workflow-executor.ts else branch at line 947 handled completed and failed status identically. resolveCompletion() ran unconditionally when spec.completion existed. Static completion.message strings like "Handoff captured to .project/handoff.json" fired on failure even though nothing was written. All workflow specs with completion messages assumed success — none checked ${{ status }}. formatResult() already correctly renders failure but was bypassed when spec.completion was present.
issues.resolved_by: v0.11.3 commit b614be4 — added state.status === "completed" guard to completion condition

issues.title: Agent output instructions tell agents to write files they cannot write — fenced JSON rejected
issues.body: buildPrompt() in step-shared.ts told agents "Write your result as valid JSON to: <path>" but 14 of 16 JSON-producing agents have tools: [read] or similar — no write capability. Their only output channel is textOutput (last assistant message). No instruction prohibited markdown fences, so LLMs wrapped JSON in markdown code blocks which JSON.parse() rejected. The prompt instruction was mismatched with agent capabilities.
issues.resolved_by: v0.11.3 commit b614be4 — rewritten to "raw JSON only, no markdown fences" with file-write as secondary

issues.title: Format-json output path silently swallows JSON parse failures — step completes with output: undefined
issues.body: step-agent.ts lines 232-238: the format-json path (no schema) caught JSON.parse exceptions and silently left output undefined. Step completed successfully with no output. persistStepOutput then wrote { text: garbage } to the output file. Downstream steps accessing steps.X.output.field got undefined — cascading failures untraceable to the silent swallow. Block writes are fatal on validation failure; this path was not, violating the principle from dec-009 that ungated paths next to gated ones are not enforcement.
issues.resolved_by: v0.11.3 commit b614be4 — parse failure now marks step as failed, same contract as schema-bound path

issues.title: Classify prompt lacks conversation history — monitors produce false positives on multi-turn backreferences
issues.body: The classify prompt receives user_text and assistant_text from the current turn only. When a user message backreferences prior turns ('reoutput my original task', 'as I said earlier'), the classifier cannot verify whether the response addresses the referenced content. It sees a short user request and a long technical response, pattern-matches to 'deviation', and flags incorrectly. Observed: 3 false positives in a single session from the hedge monitor, all on responses that directly addressed what the user asked. Root cause: no conversation_history collector exists. The classifier reasons in a single-turn vacuum. Fix: add a conversation_history context collector that provides the last N turns, or expand user_text to include referenced prior messages.
issues.resolved_by: conversation_history collector with adaptive window — regex-based referential detection, 1-3 turn summaries, 2000 char budget

issues.title: No TUI visibility into monitor token usage and cost — side-channel LLM calls are invisible
issues.body: Every monitor classification is an LLM call (classify model, typically claude-sonnet). These calls consume tokens and incur cost but are completely invisible in the TUI. No running tally, no per-monitor breakdown, no session total. In a session with active monitors firing on every turn_end, the accumulated cost can be significant. Users have no way to see what monitors are costing them. Need: running tally in TUI showing monitor token usage (input/output) and cost, updated after each classification call.

issues.title: No state coherence monitor — project blocks drift from source as development proceeds
issues.body: Project block data (architecture line counts, domain entry counts, version references, conventions test counts) goes stale as code changes accumulate. No monitor watches commits and compares block assertions against source truth. The block API supports programmatic writes, the monitor system supports write actions on FLAG, and block-to-source mappings are deterministic — a state coherence monitor class could detect staleness on commit and write corrections directly without steering. Requires: project_blocks context collector, block-to-source mapping pattern library, classify template that reasons about state coherence rather than behavioral patterns, write action that updates stale fields.

issues.title: Hedge monitor classifies tool-call-heavy responses as empty — tool_results not in classify context
issues.body: The hedge monitor's classify context includes tool_calls (names of tools called) but not tool_results (what those tools produced). When the assistant's response is primarily tool work — edits, writes, commits, builds — the text portion is a brief summary. The classifier sees tool names and a terse text response, concludes 'the assistant provided no response', and flags incorrectly. Observed: 'The assistant provided no response at all' on a turn that edited files, ran builds, and committed. The work was in tool results the classifier never saw. Fix: add tool_results to the hedge monitor's classify.context array, or teach the classify prompt that tool_calls represent substantive work even when assistant_text is brief.
issues.resolved_by: commit 2745cf9 — tool_results added to hedge classify context, classify template updated to treat tool results as evidence of substantive work

issues.title: Skill generator cannot introspect validator phases or SDK query functions — new capabilities require narrative updates
issues.body: The skill generator introspects tools, commands, events, shortcuts, and resource directories. It cannot introspect internal function behavior — validator phases and SDK query functions are invisible. When new validation checks or SDK capabilities are added, they only surface in the skill if the narrative (skill-narrative.md) is manually updated. The agent vocabulary extractor (extractAgentVocabulary) proved the pattern: export a registry, generator reads it, produces a vocabulary section. The validator now exports validationChecks() returning 11 check descriptors (commit 6fc1611), and the SDK exports agentContracts() and agentsByBlock(). The registries exist — what remains is generator integration: add extractValidationVocabulary() that calls validationChecks() and produces a <validation_vocabulary> section, parallel to <agent_vocabulary>, <planning_vocabulary>, and <monitor_vocabulary>.
issues.resolved_by: commit 4dffeec — extractValidationVocabulary() added to generator, produces <validation_vocabulary> table from validationChecks() registry

issues.title: contextBlocks injection only reads static files — no support for computed blocks derived at dispatch time
issues.body: compileAgentSpec() reads .project/<name>.json for each contextBlocks entry. Blocks whose content is derivable from code (architecture, domain reference entries) go stale between writes. The injection path should support registered generator functions alongside static files — for derivable blocks, the generator runs at injection time, returning the same shape as the static block. Agent templates and shared macros are unaffected — they render whatever data they receive regardless of source.

issues.title: Architecture block is manually maintained — modules, dependencies, and patterns are derivable from package.json and source
issues.body: The architecture block restates what code inspection provides: module names from package.json, entry files, dependencies, responsibility from package descriptions. These go stale on every code change. A projectArchitecture(cwd) function could derive modules and dependencies from package.json files and entry points, producing the same schema shape as the static block. This would make the architecture block a computed block candidate for issue-020.

issues.title: Domain block reference entries duplicate SDK query results — agent counts, workflow counts, step types go stale
issues.body: Domain entries like agent-spec ('26 bundled agents'), workflow-spec ('15 bundled workflows'), and step type descriptions restate what availableAgents(), availableWorkflows(), and stepTypes() return dynamically. When agents or workflows are added, the domain entries are wrong. These reference entries should be computed from SDK functions at injection time rather than maintained as static JSON. Human-authored domain entries (design patterns, composition families) remain static.

issues.title: Monitor classify calls produce no debug output — misfires are uninspectable
issues.body: classifyViaAgent() calls complete() via the phantom tool pattern, extracts ToolCall.arguments, and discards all intermediate state. When a monitor misfires, there is no way to see: (1) the rendered classify prompt sent to the LLM, (2) the raw LLM response (content array, stopReason, errorMessage, usage), (3) the collected context values each collector resolved to, (4) the verdict mapping decision. The diagnostic string added in v0.14.3 (stopReason + content types + errorMessage) helps for API-level failures but not for classification quality issues (wrong verdict, false positive). Part of the broader debugger layer (issue-036): monitor session capture is the monitor-specific instantiation of execution tracing. Side-car JSONL is the correct pattern — monitor logs are a parallel audit trail, not conversation entries. Location: .workflows/monitors/<monitor-name>/ or similar project-owned path (not .pi/ which is pi's territory). Extensions get ReadonlySessionManager — cannot write to main session, which is correct since classify calls are side-channel. The completeAgent() refactor (monitors-as-agents design) should build session capture into the shared execution function so both workflow steps and monitors get it from the same path.

issues.title: Classify calls with thinking enabled return empty text — model puts verdict in thinking block
issues.body: classifyPrompt() passes thinkingEnabled: true to complete(). With thinking enabled, the model can place its entire response (including the CLEAN/FLAG/NEW verdict) inside the thinking block and return no text content. extractText() filters for type === 'text' only, so thinking content is invisible. parseVerdict('') hits the unrecognized format path and returns verdict: 'error'. Observed on fragility monitor: 'classify failed: Unrecognized verdict format: ""'. Immediate fix: extractText() should fall back to thinking block content when no text blocks are present. This issue is also a symptom of issue-025 — unifying the classify path with agent specs eliminates this bug class entirely since the agent output pipeline already handles thinking extraction.
issues.resolved_by: extractResponseText() replaces extractText() in classifyPrompt() — falls back to thinking block content when no text parts present. Agent path uses extractResponseText() natively. Both paths now handle thinking-only responses.

issues.title: Monitor classify calls reinvent agent infrastructure — should use agent specs for LLM call contracts
issues.body: Monitor classify calls hand-roll template rendering, context injection, model resolution, and output parsing that the agent spec system already provides declaratively. Monitors have their own createMonitorTemplateEnv(), collectors parallel contextBlocks, parseVerdict() hand-parses what output.schema validates, and parseModelSpec() reimplements model registry resolution. A monitor's classify call IS an agent call — structured input, prompt template, model, structured output. Direction: monitors declare classify.agent referencing a .agent.yaml that defines the LLM call contract, executed in-process via direct complete() rather than subprocess dispatch. One spec format for all LLM calls, monitor-specific concerns (collectors, patterns, actions, ceiling) stay in .monitor.json, verdict format becomes output.schema with enum validation. Eliminates issue-024 (thinking block extraction) as a side effect since agent output pipeline already handles it. The agent spec parser and template compiler are the only pieces needed from pi-workflows — they could live in pi-project or a small shared package without moving the entire workflow engine. Rejected alternative: extracting shared infrastructure from pi-workflows into pi-project is unbounded refactoring that defers the design question behind a prerequisite wall.
issues.resolved_by: classifyViaAgent() uses createAgentLoader + compileAgentSpec + in-process complete(). Per-monitor .agent.yaml specs define model, thinking, output schema, task template. Classify templates gain {% if json_output %} conditional. mapVerdictToClassifyResult() replaces text parsing. Legacy path preserved for monitors without classify.agent.

issues.title: Structured output via tool-use enforcement for monitor classify
issues.body: Monitor classify calls relied on prompt instructions ('respond with JSON only') and text parsing (JSON.parse on free-form LLM response). The model could ignore the instruction and produce prose preambles, reasoning text, or malformed output — causing classify failures. Fixed in classifyViaAgent() by defining VERDICT_TOOL with TypeBox parameters, passing it in Context.tools, and setting toolChoice to force the tool call. The model produces ToolCall.arguments matching the schema — no free-form text, no JSON.parse() of LLM output. Scoped to monitors (direct complete() calls). Workflow agent steps use subprocess dispatch via pi --mode json where tool-use enforcement would require pi-coding-agent core changes — that remains a separate concern.
issues.resolved_by: VERDICT_TOOL + toolChoice enforcement in classifyViaAgent(), ToolCall extraction replaces extractResponseText() + JSON.parse() + parseVerdict() fallback

issues.title: extractResponseText() uses wrong property name for ThinkingContent — thinking block extraction was broken since implementation
issues.body: extractResponseText() checked 'text' in part for thinking blocks, but pi-ai ThinkingContent has { type: 'thinking', thinking: string }, not { type: 'thinking', text: string }. The fallback to thinking content never fired. Test fixtures used { type: 'thinking', text: '...' } — a made-up shape that matched the code's assumption, not the real type. Tests passed against wrong fixtures. Fixed in fd02c15 — property corrected to 'thinking', test fixtures corrected. Root cause: implementing agent was not scoped with the exact ThinkingContent type signature.
issues.resolved_by: fd02c15 — corrected property access from .text to .thinking, fixed test fixtures to use real ThinkingContent shape

issues.title: Agent steps should declare block write-back targets — memory accumulates as side effect of work, not separate task
issues.body: Currently an agent step produces output (StepOutputSpec in types.ts:114 — format, schema, path) but persisting results to project blocks requires a separate block step wired manually in the workflow YAML. This means memory accumulation is an authoring burden, not automatic. Extension: add an optional output.block field to StepOutputSpec (e.g., output.block: decisions) that tells the workflow executor to appendToBlock() or updateItemInBlock() after the agent returns. The block API (block-api.ts) handles validated writes. The schema comes from .project/schemas/<blockName>.schema.json. The agent doesn't know it's building memory — the step spec declares the write target, the executor persists it. Domain-agnostic: a sales agent writes to a leads block, a code agent writes to a decisions block, a review agent writes to a findings block. The write-back runs after output validation, using the same atomic write path as the block step type (step-block.ts).

issues.title: Artifact format rendering — produce editable surfaces (markdown, CSV, HTML) from schema-validated block data
issues.body: Workflow artifacts (ArtifactSpec in types.ts:18 — path, from, schema) write JSON files to disk. Block data is structured and schema-validated but only machine-readable. Extension: add a format field to ArtifactSpec (markdown, csv, html, yaml alongside json). The executor calls a format renderer after schema validation: JSON data → rendered artifact in the declared format. The shared macros system (templates/shared/macros.md) already renders block data as markdown — render_project, render_architecture, render_tasks, render_conventions, etc. The same pattern extends to CSV (tabular blocks like tasks, issues) and HTML (dashboards, reports). The rendered artifact is a view of the block data, editable in the format's native tool (spreadsheet opens CSV, browser opens HTML, editor opens markdown). The block remains the source of truth; the artifact is a rendered surface. This is the 'editable artifacts' rubric item from agent theory: outcomes require surfaces you can edit. The system is domain-agnostic — a tasks block rendered as CSV is a project management spreadsheet; an issues block rendered as HTML is a bug tracker dashboard; a verification block rendered as markdown is a progress report. The block schema defines the data contract; the format renderer defines the presentation. Users work on the surface; the block persists the truth. Note: artifacts are not coding-process outputs — the framework is outcome-agnostic. An artifact is any verifiable output from an agent or workflow: an updated spreadsheet, a lesson plan, research findings, a project report, a compliance audit, a meeting summary. The artifact format system must serve any domain, not just software development.

issues.title: Writeback monitor — observe agent execution results and persist structured summaries to project blocks
issues.body: Monitors currently classify agent behavior (hedge, fragility, etc.) and write findings to the issues block via executeWriteAction() (index.ts:1312) with merge: append/upsert. Extension: a writeback monitor that fires on agent_end for any workflow step, reads the step's structured output, and determines what to record — decisions made, issues resolved, verification evidence, execution metadata. The classify call determines what category of result the agent produced. The write action persists it to the corresponding block (decisions, verification, issues, tasks). This closes the feedback loop: workflow reads blocks via contextBlocks → agent executes → writeback monitor observes output → monitor writes results back to blocks → next execution reads richer blocks. Each cycle compounds context. The monitor spec format, event system, classify.agent pipeline, and executeWriteAction with merge: upsert all exist. The new component is the writeback monitor spec, its classifier agent YAML, and the mapping from agent output categories to block write targets.

issues.title: Scheduled workflow re-execution — time-based or event-based triggers for the compounding context loop
issues.body: Workflows execute once (or resume from checkpoint). The compounding context loop requires re-execution: run workflow → blocks updated by writeback monitor → re-run workflow with richer blocks. Currently the user must manually invoke /workflow run each time. Extension: a schedule field on WorkflowSpec (cron expression or interval) that triggers re-execution. The loop step type (LoopSpec in types.ts:56) already supports maxAttempts and iteration. A scheduled workflow is a loop at a higher level — the workflow itself iterates on a time basis, reading progressively richer blocks each cycle. Implementation could be a schedule step type, a workflow-level cron field, or an external trigger that calls the workflow tool. The executor already handles checkpoint/resume and state persistence to .workflows/runs/. The scheduling layer adds when to re-execute, not how.

issues.title: Tool-use structured output for workflow agent steps that declare output.schema — in-process dispatch for schema-bound non-tool-using agents
issues.body:
Workflow agent steps with output.schema or output.format: json dispatch via subprocess (dispatch.ts:162, spawn('pi', ['--mode', 'json', ...])). The LLM call happens inside pi-coding-agent, not in our code. We can't control the complete() call parameters. The subprocess agent receives a prompt that says 'respond with JSON only' and the model may or may not comply. step-agent.ts:205-241 validates post-hoc via JSON.parse(), failing the step on malformed output.

The mechanism for guaranteed structured output: the Anthropic API supports 'phantom tool' calls — define a Tool with the output schema as its TypeBox parameters, pass it in Context.tools, set toolChoice: { type: 'tool', name: '<name>' }. The LLM is forced to produce a ToolCall response whose arguments match the schema. The tool is never executed — it exists only as a schema constraint on the API response format. The API validates the arguments before returning them. This is what issue-026 implemented for monitor classify calls.

Two paths for workflow agents: (1) Agent steps that produce structured output WITHOUT iterative tool use (analyzers, classifiers, planners — agents whose role is reasoning, not file manipulation) could dispatch in-process via complete() with the phantom tool pattern. The agent spec has the model, compileAgentSpec() renders the prompt, output.schema converts to a TypeBox tool definition. No subprocess needed. (2) Agent steps that need iterative tool use (implementers, task-workers calling read/write/bash across multiple turns) must stay subprocess. Structured output enforcement there requires pi-coding-agent to support the phantom tool pattern when the agent spec declares output.schema — that's an upstream change outside this repo.

The agent spec could declare a dispatch mode: dispatch: 'in-process' for schema-bound reasoning agents, dispatch: 'subprocess' (default) for tool-using agents. Or infer it: if agent spec has no tools field, dispatch in-process; if it has tools, dispatch via subprocess.

Impacted components: step-agent.ts (branch dispatch path based on agent spec), dispatch.ts (unchanged for subprocess agents), types.ts AgentSpec/StepSpec (dispatch mode field), agent-spec.ts parseAgentYaml() (read dispatch field), workflow-sdk.ts agentContracts() (surface dispatch mode), VALIDATION_CHECKS (warn if agent declares tools + in-process dispatch), skill generator agent vocabulary table (show dispatch mode per agent). The phantom tool definition logic (schema → TypeBox Tool → toolChoice → ToolCall extraction) should be extracted from classifyViaAgent() in pi-behavior-monitors into a shared utility importable by both packages.

issues.title: Expression-level field validation against source schemas in workflow step input blocks
issues.body:
The template-input alignment validator (template-validation.ts) traces field access in Nunjucks templates against source schemas — e.g., {{ architecture.overview }} checked against the architecture block schema. But the same field access happens in workflow step input blocks via ${{ }} expressions: input: { overview: '${{ steps.load.output.architecture.overview }}' }. The validator checks that steps.load exists and that the expression is well-formed (extractExpressions in workflow-sdk.ts), but does not check that .architecture.overview is a valid field path against the source step's output schema. If the source step declares output.schema or is a block read (with traceable block schema), the validator should verify each dotted field path in ${{ }} expressions against the resolved schema — same tracing logic that template-validation already implements for Nunjucks variables.

Impacted components: validateWorkflow() in workflow-sdk.ts (new check after existing step-reference validation), extractExpressions() already parses dotted paths and field references, traceInputSchema() and loadSchemaFields() in template-validation.ts provide the schema resolution logic to reuse, VALIDATION_CHECKS array needs new entry, validationChecks() return updates automatically, skill generator <validation_vocabulary> table regenerates from registry.

issues.title: Agent templates with tool-use output enforcement should drop output format instructions — template focuses on reasoning, tool definition handles output contract
issues.body:
When tool-use structured output is active (issue-026 for monitors, issue-032 for workflow agents), the template's output instructions ('Respond with a JSON object: {...}' or 'Reply CLEAN/FLAG/NEW') become redundant — the toolChoice forces the schema shape regardless of what the template says. The template should focus purely on the classification/analysis task and reasoning guidance. Output format instructions in templates are a holdover from the prompt-based enforcement era. With tool-use, they add noise and can conflict with the tool definition (e.g., template says 'respond with JSON' but tool forces a different field set). The classify templates already have {% if json_output %} conditionals from the issue-025 migration. The fix: when tool-use enforcement is active, templates should not include output format instructions at all. The template rendering context should signal this (e.g., tool_enforced: true) so templates can omit the instructions block entirely. For workflow agent templates, the same principle applies: buildPrompt() in step-shared.ts:95-106 appends output instructions ('Your final response MUST be raw JSON only'). When tool-use dispatch is active, these instructions should be omitted.

Impacted components: buildPrompt() in step-shared.ts (skip output instructions when phantom tool active), all classify.md templates in pi-behavior-monitors/examples/ (remove {% if json_output %} blocks), compileAgentSpec() template context (add tool_enforced flag).

issues.title: Per-monitor configurable collector parameters — context window tunable from YAML without code changes
issues.body: Collector limits (conversation_history turns, tool_results count, tool_calls count, truncation char limits) are hardcoded in collector functions in index.ts. The monitor YAML declares which collectors to use (classify.context array) but not their parameters. Extension: classify.context becomes an object map with optional per-collector config — e.g., context: { conversation_history: { turns: 5 }, tool_results: { limit: 10, truncate: 3000 }, tool_calls: { limit: 30 } }. The collector functions already accept optional limit parameters (collectToolResults has limit=5, collectToolCalls has limit=20). The plumbing exists — the missing piece is reading per-collector config from the monitor spec and passing it through classifyViaAgent to the collector calls. Falls back to current defaults when config is absent. Changes take effect on next monitor firing after /reload. This is part of the live tuning loop: monitor fires → produces verdict → user/LLM evaluates quality → adjusts parameters from within the session → next firing reflects the adjustment. The README principle applies: 'the use of the tool shows you the shape of how to make the version of the tool that you want.' Collector parameters are the primary tuning surface for classification quality — they determine how much context the model sees, which determines verdict accuracy. Issue-038 adds the command-mediated interface for changing these parameters; this issue adds the spec-level support.

issues.title: Execution trace debugger — capture full input→template→LLM→parse→result chain for workflow steps and monitor classify calls
issues.body: When a workflow step fails, a monitor misclassifies, or a block write is rejected, nothing captures the full execution chain: what input arrived, what the template rendered to, what the LLM received as prompt, what it produced, how the response was parsed, where it diverged from the expected schema. Issue-023 covers monitor session capture specifically. This issue is broader: a debugger layer that captures execution traces for all agent invocations — workflow steps (via dispatch subprocess session logs, already partially captured in .workflows/runs/sessions/), monitor classify calls (no capture currently), and block validation failures (error message only, no input context). The debugger surface should be queryable via SDK functions (e.g., stepTrace(runId, stepName), classifyTrace(monitorName, timestamp)) and inspectable via tools so the LLM can self-diagnose failures without the user needing to read raw log files. This is the missing fractal element: SDK describes capability, validator describes correctness, monitors describe behavior, debugger describes execution.

issues.title: SDK query surface for execution history — lastRunResult, stepTrace, executionHistory functions
issues.body: projectState() shows current state. validateWorkflow() shows spec correctness. Nothing shows what happened in past executions. Run state lives in .workflows/runs/ as JSON files (state.json per run, session JSONL per step) but there's no SDK function to query it. An LLM can't inspect what went wrong without reading raw JSON files and knowing the directory structure. Needed: executionHistory(cwd, workflowName?) → list of runs with status/timing/usage summaries. lastRunResult(cwd, workflowName) → most recent run's state. stepTrace(cwd, runId, stepName) → full step result including session log path. These derive from existing .workflows/runs/ data — no new persistence needed, just query functions over what's already written. Follows the SDK pattern: derive dynamically from filesystem, add to workflow-sdk.ts exports, surface via /workflow status and the skill generator.

issues.title: Monitor tuning tools — command-mediated parameter changes for collector config, classify template, agent YAML
issues.body: Patterns and instructions are tunable via /<monitor-name> <text> commands. But collector parameters (issue-035), classify template prose, and agent YAML fields are not tunable via commands — the LLM must use raw file edits and know Nunjucks syntax and YAML format. For the system to be self-tuning from live enactment, every parameter that affects classification quality should be adjustable via tool or command. Examples: /monitors hedge context conversation_history.turns=5 (adjust collector window), /monitors hedge template (show current classify template for inspection), /monitors hedge model claude-sonnet-4-6 (change classify model). The /monitors command infrastructure exists (monitors-control, monitors-rules tools). Extension: add parameter-setting subcommands that write to the monitor spec JSON and agent YAML, taking effect on next /reload or next firing. The skill should document these commands so the LLM knows they exist.

issues.title: Monitor spec validator — validateMonitor() checking spec + agent YAML + classify template against schemas and available collectors
issues.body: validateWorkflow() checks workflow YAML specs at authoring time — agent resolution, schema existence, template-input alignment, step references, etc. No equivalent exists for monitor specs. An LLM authoring a monitor gets no feedback until runtime. validateMonitor(spec, cwd) would check: classify.agent resolves to an existing .agent.yaml, the agent YAML's prompt.task.template resolves in the template search paths, classify.context entries are valid collector names (from COLLECTOR_DESCRIPTORS), the verdict.schema.json exists at the agent's output.schema path, pattern and instruction files exist. Returns { status, issues[] } matching ValidationResult. Exposed via /monitors validate [name] command and monitors-validate tool. Added to the VALIDATION_CHECKS registry. The skill generator surfaces it in the validation vocabulary table.

issues.title: Skill generator coverage — surface classify template variables, collector output shapes, tuning commands, debug query paths
issues.body: The generated SKILL.md surfaces tools, commands, vocabulary tables (collectors, when conditions, verdicts), agent contracts (inputSchema, contextBlocks, outputFormat). It does not surface: what Nunjucks variables each classify template expects (patterns, instructions, iteration, plus each collector name), what each collector returns and in what format (e.g., tool_results returns '---\n[toolName] result\n---' blocks), what tuning commands are available per monitor (issue-038), how to inspect execution traces and debug failures (issue-036/037). Without these in the skill, the LLM can use monitors but can't tune them or debug them — it hits a wall where self-correction requires knowledge the skill doesn't provide. Extension: the skill generator should extract template variable references from classify.md files (same Nunjucks variable extraction the workflow validator uses), document collector output formats from COLLECTOR_DESCRIPTORS (add a format field), and list available tuning/debug commands.

issues.title: Scoped/filtered contextBlocks reads — agents cannot request subsets of block data
issues.body: Current contextBlocks injection reads whole blocks from .project/ and injects them into the template environment. Agents cannot express 'inject only Active requirements', 'inject only verification entries for upstream slices', 'inject only decisions related to the active milestone'. This is the missing primitive for gsd-2's Forward Intelligence pattern, dependency slice summaries, and requirement-scoped research. Extension: contextBlocks entries should accept a filter expression or query (e.g., contextBlocks: [{name: 'verification', where: 'slice_id in ${{ dependencies }}'}] or contextBlocks: [{name: 'requirements', where: 'status == Active'}]). Reuses the existing ${{ }} expression engine for consistency. Without this, agents either get all-or-nothing block data (wasted tokens, irrelevant content) or must be hand-wired per use case.

issues.title: Token budgeting across DAG edges — upstream output is interpolated verbatim with no budget
issues.body: When step B references ${{ steps.A.textOutput }} or ${{ steps.A.output }}, the entire upstream content is interpolated into B's input with no budget, no truncation, and no priority between direct and transitive dependencies. A 50KB upstream textOutput becomes 50KB in the downstream prompt regardless of token cost or relevance. context-packet solves this with: always-include summaries, body-first truncation from most distant upstream, truncated flag on result. reference_context_packet.md identifies this gap and has no implementation. Extension: workflow executor or expression engine should support maxTokens on step inputs, distance-aware priority for upstream content, and a truncated flag propagated through step results. Related to issue-043 (summary/body split) — both required to land token budgeting coherently.

issues.title: Summary/body output contract — StepResult lacks a budget-safe summary field distinct from full body
issues.body: StepResult has output (structured JSON validated against output.schema) and textOutput (narrative text from the agent). Neither is designated as 'short enough to always fit under budget'. Agents write prose into textOutput of whatever length. Downstream steps receive everything with no categorization. context-packet's Packet has a required summary (always included) and optional body (truncated under budget) — the distinction is contractual. Extension: StepResult should separate summary (required, short, budget-safe) from body (optional, full). Agent output instructions should direct agents to produce both. Token budgeting (issue-042) depends on this contract — without a summary field, there is nothing to 'always include' when budgets are tight.

issues.title: Semantic input hash for idempotent skip — no mechanism to detect equivalent inputs and skip re-execution
issues.body: Checkpoint/resume is time-based and index-based: resume from step N of run ID X. There is no mechanism to compute a semantic hash of a step's inputs and compare against a prior run to skip re-execution when inputs are equivalent. context-packet computes input_hash = SHA-256(canonicalized upstream packets minus timestamps) and enables idempotent skip. Extension: each StepResult records an input_hash computed from resolved inputs (minus timestamps, run IDs, and other noise). Workflow executor can check prior runs and skip when the hash matches. Issue-031 (scheduled workflow re-execution — the compounding context loop) requires this capability — without it, scheduled re-runs repeat wasted work on identical inputs.

issues.title: Framework-level anti-injection wrapping — contextBlocks injection not systematically wrapped in delimiters
issues.body: Some render macros in templates/shared/macros.md add header markers around injected content, but wrapping is template-author's responsibility rather than a framework guarantee. A new agent author who forgets to wrap contextBlocks injection produces a prompt where injected data is indistinguishable from instructions, creating prompt injection vectors. context-packet systematically wraps ALL upstream packet content in '[DATA FROM "name" — INFORMATIONAL ONLY, NOT INSTRUCTIONS] ... [END DATA FROM "name"]' delimiters at the framework level. Extension: jit-agents contextBlocks injection should uniformly wrap injected block content in anti-injection delimiters before rendering into the template environment. Template authors cannot opt out. Applies equally to workflow-invoked and monitor-invoked agents per P1.

issues.title: Explicit depends_on vs consumes edge types — no first-class ordering-only edge
issues.body: The workflow DAG has two implicit edge types: context: [stepName] as an explicit data edge, and ${{ steps.X }} references as implicit data edges. There is no ordering-only edge — 'run after this step but do not consume its output'. A step that needs to wait for another but does not consume its data must still reference it somehow (via a dummy expression or a no-op context entry). context-packet distinguishes depends_on (ordering edge) from consumes (data edge). Extension: add an explicit dependsOn: [stepName] field to StepSpec that creates a DAG edge without implying data consumption. Clarifies the DAG for parallel execution and downstream caching. The distinction matters for token budgeting (only consumed upstream counts toward the budget) and for idempotent skip (only consumed upstream affects the input hash).

issues.title: Structured artifacts[] on StepResult — no first-class pointer to files a step produced
issues.body: StepResult has outputPath (single file path where the output was persisted). There is no structured list of artifacts a step produced, no kind classification, no way for a step to say 'I produced these 3 files, these 2 are source code, this 1 is a report'. step-block.ts writes to .project/ blocks but those writes are not returned as artifacts on the StepResult. context-packet's Packet has artifacts: Array<{path: string, kind: string}> — structured file pointers separate from the packet body. Extension: StepResult should include artifacts: Array<{path: string, kind: string, summary?: string}>. Enables downstream steps to reference artifacts by path and kind without loading them into the prompt; enables block write-back monitors (issue-030) to observe what a step produced.

issues.title: .pi/agents reads contradict README directory ownership declaration — framework code violates stated architecture
issues.body: README.md and CLAUDE.md declare '.pi/ is Pi platform territory. Neither extension writes to it.' But createAgentLoader() in packages/pi-workflows/src/agent-spec.ts:141 searches .pi/agents/ as the first tier in the agent discovery path. The search tests at packages/pi-workflows/src/workflow-sdk.test.ts reference .pi/agents/ extensively. SKILL.md documents the path. This is a structural incoherence: the declared architecture says .pi/ is Pi's namespace and nothing in our packages writes to it, but our framework code READS from .pi/agents/ as part of agent discovery. Either the declaration is wrong or the code is wrong. Extension: the declared architecture is authoritative per later README commits; createAgentLoader() must be updated to search a consumer-owned location instead of .pi/agents/. Current provisional replacement (D7 in jit-agents-spec.md): .project/agents/ → ~/.pi/agent/agents/ → package/agents/. The user-global ~/.pi/ path is a separate question — it may itself need to move.

issues.title: step-loop.ts:187 calls compileAgentSpec without cwd — agents in loop steps never receive contextBlocks data
issues.body: Disclarity audit of pi-workflows found that step-loop.ts:187 calls compileAgentSpec(agentSpec, resolvedInput, options.templateEnv) WITHOUT the cwd parameter. Without cwd, compileAgentSpec cannot read .project/ blocks for contextBlocks injection — the named blocks silently resolve to null in the template environment. Any agent that runs inside a loop step and declares contextBlocks gets null where block data was expected, producing broken prompts without any error. This is a functional bug with no current test coverage that would catch it. Contrast: step-agent.ts correctly passes cwd. Fix: step-loop.ts must pass options.cwd (or the equivalent) to compileAgentSpec. Must add a regression test exercising a loop step with an agent that declares contextBlocks.

issues.title: step-monitor.ts ignores classify.agent — workflow monitor verification gates silently return CLEAN
issues.body: packages/pi-workflows/src/step-monitor.ts is a separate lightweight monitor step executor for workflow verification gates. Its MonitorSpec interface (lines 23-37) has NO classify.agent field — only classify.prompt, classify.promptTemplate, classify.context, classify.model. It renders prompts via Nunjucks or inline string replacement and parses verdicts via TEXT parsing (parseVerdict at lines 193-206, expects 'CLEAN', 'FLAG:', 'NEW:' prefixes). It does NOT use phantom tool enforcement, does NOT use agent specs, does NOT use structured output. Issue-025 unified pi-behavior-monitors to require classify.agent on all monitor specs, but step-monitor.ts was not updated — it predates the agent-based architecture. When a workflow uses monitor: <name> and the monitor only declares classify.agent (as all unified monitors should), step-monitor.ts produces no prompt (renderPrompt returns null) and silently returns CLEAN at line 258. Workflow verification gates are therefore non-functional for any monitor that has been unified. Per P1 in jit-agents-spec.md, there should be one classify pathway across the framework — step-monitor.ts must delegate to pi-behavior-monitors' classifyViaAgent() (which in turn goes through jit-agents) rather than reimplementing classification.

issues.title: Directory-watching monitor — general filesystem observation primitive for skill discovery, dependency drift, external artifact arrival
issues.body: gsd-2's skill discovery (skill-discovery.ts) snapshots a directory at session start, detects new files by comparing against the snapshot, and injects newly discovered content into agent prompts. This pattern generalizes: any directory that may gain new files during execution (skills, dependencies, external artifacts, config changes) should be observable via a monitor. In our framework this is a directory-watching monitor that periodically scans a configured path, detects additions/changes, writes findings to a .project/ block, and triggers contextBlocks injection into subsequent agents. The general primitive enables: skill discovery (watch ~/.claude/skills/), dependency drift detection (watch node_modules/ or lock files), external artifact arrival (watch a drop directory), config change propagation. Currently no monitor collector observes filesystem state outside .project/.

issues.title: Dynamic model selection via expressions in agent spec model field
issues.body: Agent spec model field is currently a hardcoded string (e.g., model: claude-sonnet-4-6). gsd-2 has auto-model-selection.ts and capability-aware model routing (ADR-004) that select models at dispatch time based on task complexity, cost budget, and capability requirements. In our framework the agent spec model field should support expression resolution (e.g., model: ${{ registry.best(capability: 'thinking', budget: 'standard') }}) evaluated at dispatch time against the model registry. The model registry lives in pi-ai/pi-coding-agent; the dispatch decision is orchestration that belongs in the agent compilation pipeline. Without this, every agent locks to a specific model at authoring time, preventing runtime cost/quality optimization and provider failover.

issues.title: Pre-execution monitors — gate agent completion on required tool calls before final output
issues.body: gsd-2's discuss.md prompt has a 'mandatory investigation pass' — the agent MUST scout the codebase, check library docs, and web search before asking its first question. This is articulated in the prompt but not enforced by the framework. In our framework, a pre-execution monitor could gate agent completion by checking whether required tool calls have been made (e.g., 'this agent must have called grep or read at least N files before submitting final output'). The prompt encodes the intent; the monitor enforces it. This is a specific application of the monitor classify pattern: the collector observes tool_results during the agent's execution, the classifier checks whether required investigation tools were called, FLAG verdict prevents premature completion. Generalizes to any agent quality gate based on observable execution behavior before output submission.

issues.title: Per-agent tool-call budget tracking — framework-level counting and enforcement of tool-call budgets
issues.body: gsd-2's prompts articulate web search budgets ('max ~15 per session', 'Target 3-5 total web searches for a typical research unit') but leave enforcement to the LLM's self-discipline. The framework has no mechanism to count tool calls by type across agent invocations or enforce limits. This is the tool-call counterpart to issue-042 (token budgeting across DAG edges): who counts calls, where is the count stored, how is over-budget handled. In our framework this is either a monitor collector that tracks tool_results counts by tool name and classifies over-budget as FLAG, or a first-class budget field on the agent spec (e.g., budget: { web_search: 5, grep: unlimited }) enforced by the dispatch runtime. Without this, agents either self-regulate (unreliable) or have no budget at all (wasteful).

issues.title: Framework gap FGAP-001 — pi-project does not support hierarchical/nested block storage
issues.body: block-api.ts assumes one flat JSON file per block. There is no way to express a nested decomposition like roadmap → milestone → epic → story → task as a directory tree; work decomposition must be smuggled into a single flat array with denormalized parent references, losing scope ownership and forcing the global tasks.json to conflate work across all levels. The L3 Work layer of the Muni five-layer model (see .project/layer-plans.json PLAN-001) cannot be natively represented until readBlock / writeBlock / appendToBlock gain subpath support and availableBlocks gains nested-directory discovery. Hand-enacted today via flat features.json with stories and tasks embedded as arrays — features/{id}.json per feature is the target once this gap closes. Canonical owner: .project/framework-gaps.json FGAP-001. Related artifact: FEAT-001 STORY-004/005 (consumer migration) which operates on the flat-block substrate until this lands. Canonical vocabulary: hierarchical work breakdown structure (WBS); nested aggregate storage.

issues.title: Framework gap FGAP-002 — no per-scope finding registries on block kinds
issues.body: There is no convention for a block to carry its own embedded findings array distinct from global issues.json. A design issue discovered during review of jit-agents-spec.md has nowhere to live except the global issue tracker, where it is indistinguishable from task-level defects and feature-level scope conflicts. Result: issues.json (this file, currently 65+ entries) conflates L2 architectural contradictions with L4 implementation bugs with L5 incident-derived rules. The hand-enacted .project/spec-reviews.json and .project/features.json already embed scoped findings arrays as a preview of the target shape — block-api needs to standardize the convention and provide appendFinding(blockName, parentItemId, finding). Global issues.json becomes a derived view (see issue-057 / FGAP-003). Canonical owner: .project/framework-gaps.json FGAP-002. Canonical vocabulary: scoped issues; per-artifact defect list; embedded finding registries.

issues.title: Framework gap FGAP-003 — no materialized views over scoped blocks
issues.body: projectState() in project-sdk.ts does in-memory aggregation but it is not a persisted, addressable, schema-validated block. There is no way to declare 'global issues_view.json is a derived index over per-scope finding registries' or 'open_work_view.json aggregates all non-terminal stories across features'. Consumers wanting a flat queryable view (the current issues.json shape) cannot have it alongside scope-owned registries without manual sync. Proposed resolution: add a view-block kind with a definition file naming source blocks, a projection function, and refresh triggers; block-api exposes readView(name) but disallows direct writes; refresh fires on underlying block writes. Canonical owner: .project/framework-gaps.json FGAP-003. Canonical vocabulary: materialized view (database canon). Depends on FGAP-002 (issue-056) landing first to have scope-owned registries to project from.

issues.title: Framework gap FGAP-004 — no authorship attestation at block write time
issues.body: Only issues.schema.json (this block) has a source enum (human | agent | monitor | workflow); tasks.schema.json has assigned_agent which is a different concept (assignment, not authorship). No block records created_by / modified_by / created_at / modified_at consistently. block-api.ts does not stamp authorship on writes. The authorship-matrix principle — 'only the user can advance a finding from proposed to decided' — cannot be enforced because the writer identity is not recorded. State-machine validation of user-gated transitions (decision enacted, finding decided, feature approved) is structurally impossible without this. Proposed resolution: block-api.ts stamps created_by / modified_by / created_at / modified_at on every write, reading writer identity from a DispatchContext parameter; every block schema adds these as required fields; cross-block validation enforces authority-matrix rules per-transition. Hand-enacted today in .project/decisions.json, features.json, framework-gaps.json, spec-reviews.json, layer-plans.json — every item carries explicit created_by and created_at fields populated manually. Canonical owner: .project/framework-gaps.json FGAP-004. Canonical vocabulary: authorship attestation; written_by attribution; audit trail.

issues.title: Framework gap FGAP-005 — no state-machine validation on enum-field transitions
issues.body: AJV draft-07 validates current-value membership on enum fields but cannot express allowed transitions. Every block schema today permits any enum value on any write. 'Issue status can only go from open to in-progress, not back to open' is not expressible. 'Finding decided-state transitions require user authority' is not expressible. The whole class of lifecycle-gated workflows (design review clean-gate, decision enacted gate, feature approval gate) cannot be enforced by schema. Current workaround is discipline — which fails silently when agents run unattended. The five new block schemas landed this session (.project/schemas/decisions.schema.json, spec-reviews.schema.json, features.schema.json, framework-gaps.schema.json, layer-plans.schema.json) all carry x-lifecycle metadata at root declaring allowed transitions with required_authority annotations — this metadata is load-bearing for the framework-level implementation that will eventually honor it. Proposed resolution: add a custom AJV keyword that the schema-validator reads; block-api.ts passes both prior-value and new-value to the validator at update time; validator checks allowed transitions and required-authority; fails write if transition is invalid or authority is insufficient. Depends on FGAP-004 (issue-058) for the authorship data. Canonical owner: .project/framework-gaps.json FGAP-005. Canonical vocabulary: state-machine validation; lifecycle invariant; transition guard.

issues.title: Framework gap FGAP-006 — no schema versioning or evolution protocol
issues.body: Schemas have no version field, no $id, no migration story, no $ref composition, no shared-fragment reuse. Schemas drift silently across projects and across time. A schema rename or field rename has no backward-compatibility path. decisions.schema.json and rationale.schema.json duplicate fields with no shared fragment — a rename in one does not propagate. CLAUDE.md says 'All schemas are user-customizable' with no mention of migration or version compatibility. Long-term schema evolution is undefined. Proposed resolution: add a required $id (URI) and version field on every schema; schema-validator reads version and can apply migration functions; introduce a schema-migrations registry; $ref fragment composition becomes the standard way to express shared fields (e.g. a common 'authorship-fragment.schema.json' imported by every block schema). Canonical owner: .project/framework-gaps.json FGAP-006. Canonical vocabulary: schema evolution; JSON Schema $id; schema registry; schema versioning.

issues.title: Framework gap FGAP-007 — no staleness engine for research blocks
issues.body: Research entries (per the design in analysis/research-blocks-design.md) must carry grounding metadata (version-pinned dependencies, retrieved revisions, dated external refs) and stale_conditions (e.g. 'pi-ai upgrades past 0.63.x'). Staleness must currently be checked by hand. The framework cannot automatically fire stale_conditions against the current state of installed dependencies, git revisions, or external-URL content hashes — and cannot propagate invalidation downstream through 'informs' edges to flag affected decisions as needing re-grounding. Proposed resolution: add a staleness-engine runtime that reads every research entry's stale_conditions and compares them against the current state on a schedule or on-demand; transitions stale entries to status=stale; walks informs edges to emit structured diagnostics for any enacted decision whose supporting research has gone stale. Depends on the research block kind itself existing (research.json + research.schema.json), which is currently specified in analysis/research-blocks-design.md but not yet enacted. Canonical owner: to be added to .project/framework-gaps.json when the research block kind lands. Canonical vocabulary: grounding decay; citation rot; evidence expiry.
issues.resolved_by: Filed as FGAP-007 in framework-gaps.json (this commit). Issue mirror retired; canonical gap carries the staleness-engine work envelope.

issues.title: Monitor provider-pin — parseModelSpec defaults bare model ids to provider='anthropic'
issues.body: packages/pi-behavior-monitors/index.ts:1091-1097 implements parseModelSpec such that bare model ids (e.g. 'claude-sonnet-4-6') default to provider='anthropic'. This structural pin means monitor classify calls attempt Anthropic-direct authentication regardless of the main session's configured provider. Under OpenRouter-only auth (the user's current configuration), every monitor's classify call fails with 'Expected either apiKey or authToken to be set' because the Anthropic SDK receives undefined credentials. All five bundled classifier agent YAMLs (hedge, commit-hygiene, fragility, work-quality, unauthorized-action) declare bare model ids and are all affected. This issue is gated on three open decisions in .project/decisions.json: DEC-0001 (agent spec model-field semantics), DEC-0002 (thinking-seam enforcement), DEC-0003 (parseModelSpec ownership at the execute boundary). Per mandate-004, the fix is NOT to set ANTHROPIC_API_KEY alongside — that leaves the coupling in place. The fix lives in .project/features.json FEAT-001 as STORY-001/003 of the consumer migration arc: remove the anthropic default, move parseModelSpec into pi-jit-agents, resolve bare ids via ExtensionContext lookup. Canonical owner: FEAT-001 + DEC-0001 + DEC-0003. Operational incident: analysis/openrouter-pi-mono-setup.md.

issues.title: Dead 'thinking: on' config in five bundled classifier agent YAMLs — silently dropped by classify path
issues.body: All five bundled classifier agent YAMLs in packages/pi-behavior-monitors/agents/ (hedge-classifier, commit-hygiene-classifier, fragility-classifier, work-quality-classifier, unauthorized-action-classifier) declare 'thinking: on' in their spec. The classify path at packages/pi-behavior-monitors/index.ts:1235-1248 does NOT forward thinking to the complete() call — the parameter object passed to complete() omits the thinking field entirely. The comment at index.ts:1231 acknowledges that the Anthropic API rejects thinking + forced toolChoice, so thinking must stay off when the dispatch uses a phantom-tool verdict shape. Net effect: YAML config is dead — agents authors writing new classifier YAMLs will repeat the mistake. Gated on DEC-0002 (.project/decisions.json) which proposes that executeAgent in pi-jit-agents enforce thinking=off when toolChoice.type === 'tool' is present and emit a structured diagnostic warning. Bundled YAML updates are STORY-006 of FEAT-001 in .project/features.json. Canonical owner: FEAT-001 + DEC-0002.

issues.title: pi-workflows dispatch.ts lacks per-step provider/model override
issues.body: packages/pi-workflows/src/dispatch.ts spawns 'pi --mode json' subprocesses without passing --provider or --model flags. Workflow agent steps inherit the parent session's configured model — there is no way to route one step to OpenRouter while the rest of the workflow runs on Anthropic direct, or to pin a specific reasoning step to a higher-capability model. This parallels the monitor-level issue-062 (provider-pin in pi-behavior-monitors) but for workflow steps. The underlying question is the same: agent spec 'model:' field semantics, resolved by DEC-0001. STORY-003 TASK-003-03 in FEAT-001 includes an audit of pi-workflows/src/ for equivalent parseModelSpec implementations so any consolidation happens alongside the monitor-side fix. Beyond consolidation, enabling per-step provider choice requires either: (a) workflow step type carries provider/model fields that dispatch.ts plumbs through to pi invocation flags, or (b) workflow steps declare their ideal provider/model via the agent spec's 'model:' field and inherit ExtensionContext-resolved semantics per DEC-0001. Canonical owner: FEAT-001 STORY-003 + follow-up capability work once DEC-0001 decides.

issues.title: pi-behavior-monitors write-action path bypasses block-api and drifts silently from destination block schemas
issues.body:
executeWriteAction at packages/pi-behavior-monitors/index.ts:1316-1372 writes to .project/ blocks via raw fs.readFileSync / fs.writeFileSync, completely bypassing pi-project's block-api.ts (writeBlock, appendToBlock, updateItemInBlock) and schema-validator.ts. The writeCfg.schema field declared in monitor.json action blocks (e.g. 'schemas/issues.schema.json' in fragility.monitor.json and work-quality.monitor.json) is vestigial — read into writeCfg but never passed to any validator. This is a single package-level bug with multiple symptoms:

  (a) Schema drift uncaught at write time. The fragility and work-quality monitors ship with write templates that produce entries missing four required fields (title, body, location, package) and using enum values outside the schema (category: 'fragility', priority: '{severity}' → 'warning'). Evidence: the orphan entry fragility-fragility-mnnut4nc at .project/issues.json[35], produced by the shipped fragility.monitor.json template, which fails AJV validation against .project/schemas/issues.schema.json on all six constraints.

  (b) Vocabulary mismatch between monitor internals and issue tracker. Monitors classify with a severity vocabulary (warning/error/critical) and a monitor-name vocabulary (fragility/work-quality/hedge), but the issue-tracker schema has priority (low/medium/high/critical) and category (primitive/issue/cleanup/capability/composition). Neither vocabulary was aligned. The write template treats the two as interchangeable — they are not.

  (c) Data loss on malformed JSON. index.ts:1335-1340: 'let data = {}; try { data = JSON.parse(...) } catch { /* create fresh */ }'. If the destination file has any parse error (interrupted write, manual edit in progress, filesystem corruption), the catch silently sets data to empty and the subsequent write overwrites ALL prior entries with just the new one. There is no warning, no backup, no refusal. A single interrupted tmp+rename or manual edit can wipe issues.json.

  (d) Read-modify-write race. No inter-process locking between concurrent monitor invocations. Two monitors firing in the same window both read the same state, both append, second write loses the first write's addition.

  (e) No schema resolution. writeCfg.schema is a string like 'schemas/issues.schema.json' — but resolved against what directory? The bundled monitor examples use a relative path that would resolve differently depending on cwd. The code never resolves it at all because it never uses it, so the ambiguity is academic but documents intent that was abandoned.

Canonical resolution: executeWriteAction must route through pi-project's block-api (writeBlock/appendToBlock with name='issues' or similar) so that every write goes through schema-validator.ts with AJV. Write templates in monitor.json specs must produce entries that conform to the destination block's schema — which implies either rewriting the templates in fragility.monitor.json and work-quality.monitor.json to include title/body/location/package and map category/priority to the block's enums, OR extending issues.schema.json category and priority enums to include the monitor-produced values, OR (preferred) routing monitor findings to a scope-owned monitor-findings registry per FGAP-002 (issue-056) instead of the global issues.json. Concurrency via block-api's atomic write path (tmp + rename) with file-level locking. Data-loss protection by refusing to overwrite unparseable destination files — surface the error to the monitor caller instead of silently wiping.

Cross-references: the orphan entry (fragility-fragility-mnnut4nc) is untouched by this issue and remains at .project/issues.json[35] as evidence — deletion or migration is a separate scope decision. The monitor-side fix compounds with issue-062 (provider-pin) and issue-063 (thinking:on dead config) as the three load-bearing pi-behavior-monitors defects discovered this session. All three indicate the package's write and dispatch paths were authored before the current schema discipline landed and have drifted. Canonical owner for the structural fix: FEAT-001 consumer migration arc (.project/features.json) should absorb the executeWriteAction rewrite as a new STORY once the decision-gated work lands, OR the user may scope it as a separate follow-up epic.

issues.title: workflow executor crashes with `name.replace is not a function` on any agent dispatch
issues.body: Surfaced by the v0.24.x verification protocol run (docs/reports/pi-internal-verification-protocol-2026-05-02.md). Every agent-dispatching test section returns the runtime error `name.replace is not a function` from the workflow executor before the agent subprocess is spawned. Suspected location: packages/pi-workflows/src/workflow-executor.ts or packages/pi-workflows/src/dispatch.ts where a name-like field is normalised; investigation needed to identify the exact call site (likely a recent refactor introduced a value where a string was previously assumed). Blocks 13 of the protocol test sections (4.4, 4.5, 4.6, 4.7, 4.8, 4.10, 4.12a, 4.13c, 4.13e, 4.15, 4.16, 4.18, 5.1) and is the gating defect for REVIEW-001 dispatch via workflow (the intended shape uses an agent step then nestedAppend findings). Direct-tool surfaces (resolve-item-by-id, render-item-by-id, enforce-budget, append-block-nested-item, update-block-nested-item, remove-block-item, remove-block-nested-item, read-block-dir) all pass end-to-end so the defect is contained to the workflow executor agent-step path. One error message means one bug-shape; investigation should be bounded.
issues.location: packages/pi-workflows/src/workflow-executor.ts (or sibling agent-dispatch site — exact line TBD by investigation)

issues.title: agent-spec discovery path split — pi-workflows uses .pi/agents/, pi-jit-agents uses .project/agents/ three-tier
issues.body: Surfaced by the v0.24.x verification protocol run. The protocol authored fixture agent yamls under `.project/agents/` per the three-tier loader contract documented in pi-jit-agents (`.project/agents/` → `~/.pi/agent/agents/` → consumer builtin). Workflow agent dispatch through pi-workflows did not see them — pi-workflows still uses its own loader that points at `.pi/agents/`. Two agent-discovery code paths coexist; consumer migration to adopt pi-jit-agents createAgentLoader as the canonical surface (FEAT-001 territory) has not landed for the workflow path. The protocol workaround (copying the fixture to `.pi/agents/`) cannot be exercised because issue-066 (workflow executor crash) blocks dispatch end-to-end. Resolution belongs inside the FEAT-001 consumer-migration arc; filed here so the discovery split is queryable independent of the FEAT record’s broader scope.
issues.location: packages/pi-workflows/src/agent-spec.ts or packages/pi-workflows/src/dispatch.ts (workflow-side loader); packages/pi-jit-agents/src/agent-spec.ts (canonical three-tier loader)

issues.title: Lens curation ceremony — slash command for binning uncategorized items
issues.body: POC v2's listUncategorized() + suggestionTemplate() in analysis/poc-degree-zero-lens/render.ts demonstrate the curation surface shape — emit would-be edge-append payloads for items in the (uncategorized) bucket of a hand-curated lens. The ceremony itself — a /project lens-curate <lensId> slash command that interactively walks uncategorized items, accepts bin assignments, and translates to batched appendToBlock writes against relations.json — needs registration in pi-project's command surface. Without it, lens curation is manual edge-authoring; with it, lens curation becomes a first-class workflow. Couples to DEC-0008 (typed contextBlocks) for lens-view rendering during curation, and to DEC-0007 (composition contract) for how the curation prompt itself is composed. Aligns with analysis/2026-05-01-ceremony-ideas.md sibling commands (/project new, /project new-phase, /project edit-item, /project archive-item). Surfaced as gap #15 in the heuristic-widening pass.

issues.title: Route POC AJV instance through pi-project schema-validator's cache
issues.body: POC v2 (analysis/poc-degree-zero-lens/render.ts:77) creates its own AJV instance per run for config + relations validation, matching pi-project's schema-validator.ts ESM/CJS import shim pattern. Production form must consume the existing schema-validator cache rather than spinning parallel AJV instances per consumer. Without routing, every consumer (compile.ts lens injection, walk_descendants Nunjucks global, validateRelations gate, cluster-render) compiles its own schemas — duplicate state, duplicate compile cost, fragmented invalidation strategy. Pure integration discipline; no new mechanism needed. Surfaced as gap #20 in the POC v2 evaluation; classified as package-bound because it requires pi-project edit.

issues.title: Cross-package cache surface for config + relations + synthesized edges
issues.body: Multiple consumers want cached substrate state: compile.ts (lens injection), walk_descendants Nunjucks global, validateRelations gate, cluster-render. Three placement candidates: (a) pi-project SDK new export getProjectContext(cwd, scope) returning a memoized handle — canonical-surface aligned per DEC-0003; (b) pi-jit-agents compile-call-local — fresh per compile, no cross-package surface; (c) module-level singleton in pi-project — cwd-keyed map, mtime-invalidated. Without an explicit choice every consumer duplicates state or pays cold-cache cost. Couples to DEC-0007 (composition contract): cache strategy follows once the contract specifies what gets cached and at what scope. Issue-069 (AJV cache routing) is the schema-side analogue. Surfaced as gap #25 in the integration-map review.

issues.title: Bound synthetic-edge synthesis: lazy per-lens vs eager union
issues.body: POC v2's edgesForLens() + synthesizeFromField() unified accessor decides at query time whether synthetic edges (auto-derived lens edges from item fields) get computed. Production scale requires bounding the cardinality. Eager union — synthesizeAll(config) computing every lens's synthetic edges per call — is O(L × N) per query; for L=50 lenses and N=1000 items that's 50K synthetic edges per call. Lazy per-lens — only synthesize for the lens being queried — is O(N) per call, but requires the unified-accessor signature to know which lens is being queried. Lazy is correct for production scale; eager simpler to implement. Couples to issue-070 (cache placement) — caching synthetic edges and invalidating on item-field change is the orthogonal optimization. Surfaced as gap #26 in the integration-map review.

issues.title: Cycle handling composes between walkDescendants visited-guard and render_recursive depth-guard
issues.body: Integration map proposes templates combining walk_descendants() (Nunjucks global) with render_recursive(resolve(id), depth) per per-item-macros plan. POC v2's walkDescendants has a visited-guard preventing infinite traversal at edge-walk time. Per-item-macros' render_recursive has a depth-guard. The two guards must compose when authored relations contain cycles: walk-level visited prevents revisiting; depth-level guard prevents infinite recursion at render. Markers module (residual-debt survey item 9) emits [cycle: <id>] markers — tests against named constants. Without explicit composition, cyclic data could fail to render certain items (silent non-render) instead of emitting the cycle marker. Trace: cycle in authored edges → walk halts at visited → some items not visited → silent non-render vs marker emission. Surfaced as gap #27 in the integration-map review.

issues.title: Extend validateRelations to detect cycles in authored relations
issues.body: POC v2 validateRelations() catches dangling-id cases (edge_unresolved_child, edge_parent_wrong_block, edge_parent_not_in_bins, edge_unknown_relation_type) but does NOT detect cycles in (parent, child, relation_type) triples. Cycles in hierarchy edges create infinite drill-down for any consumer walking the hierarchy. Cycles in lens edges are nonsensical (a bin is its own item?). Validator should walk each relation_type subgraph (Tarjan or simple DFS with visited+stack) and emit edge_cycle_detected diagnostics with the cycle path. Authoring-time validation prevents the issue rather than handling it at runtime via guards (which is the issue-072 concern). Surfaced as gap #28 in the integration-map review.

issues.title: seedExamples short-circuit blocks new substrate files from reaching already-seeded projects
issues.body:
Per memory feedback_seed_examples_does_not_overwrite, seedExamples() short-circuits on existing seeded files — bundled examples updates do not propagate to seeded .pi/monitors/ in already-initialized projects. Same pattern blocks substrate-arc landings: when production lands config.json + relations.json + new schemas (config.schema.json, relations.schema.json), already-seeded projects don't get them. Two paths: (a) seedExamples gains an additive-only path that copies missing files without overwriting existing user customizations; (b) /project init gains a --migrate flag that diffs against bundled defaults and seeds missing pieces. Without one of these, every existing user must hand-copy new schemas + bootstrap config. Surfaced as gap #31 in the integration-map review.

---

DEC-0011 (enacted 2026-05-03) supersedes this issue's framing. The decision is to ship no packaged defaults; users install desired schemas and starter blocks via an opt-in mechanism. The seedExamples / additive-only / migration question is moot at the source: the artifacts that would need to be seeded or migrated are no longer shipped. This issue is reframed as 'specify and implement the opt-in install mechanism that replaces the default-copying loops at packages/pi-project/src/index.ts:215-236'. Implementation work proceeds under DEC-0011's direction, not the original seedExamples-fix framing.

issues.title: Verify writeBlock dynamic schema discovery accepts config as a typed block
issues.body: Degree-zero principle requires writeBlock(cwd, 'config', data) to validate against config.schema.json. Block-api's existing dynamic schema discovery (availableSchemas reads schemas/*.schema.json without code change) should handle this for any new block name — but it is unverified for the config-as-block case specifically. Verification: drop config.schema.json into .project/schemas/, attempt writeBlock(cwd, 'config', validData) and writeBlock(cwd, 'config', invalidData), assert valid passes and invalid throws ValidationError. If discovery does not handle the case (e.g. blockFilePath naming assumption, schema-path resolution edge case), a code change is needed. Likely a verification confirming existing behavior, but unverified is unverified. Surfaced as gap #32 in the integration-map review.

issues.title: partitions field on config block: commit to runtime semantics or drop from schema
issues.body: POC v2 config.schema.json declares partitions: [{name, blocks: [...]}] — top-level organizational divisions over the substrate. POC's render.ts reads the field but does not consume it. Integration map omits it. Two paths: (a) commit to runtime semantics — partitions scope validateProject passes (a partition's validation runs only over its declared blocks), or partitions become directory subpaths (which DEC-0009 rejects under closure-table-only direction), or partitions become the within-scope-grouping concept DEC-0010 (scope layering) might require; (b) drop from schema as dead config until a runtime use emerges. Without explicit decision, partitions sits as schema-validated noise. Couples to DEC-0009 (storage primitive direction) and DEC-0010 (scope layering). Surfaced as gap #33 in the integration-map review.
